findings.title: Docker base image not digest-pinned
findings.scenario: The installer stage base image 'node:24' is referenced by tag only. A malicious or compromised Docker Hub publisher could silently replace the tag to inject code into every build. Digest-pinning (@sha256:...) prevents this substitution.

findings.title: Docker base image not digest-pinned
findings.scenario: The final/runtime stage base image 'gcr.io/distroless/nodejs24-debian13' is referenced by tag only. Without a SHA digest, the build is not reproducible and is vulnerable to tag mutation attacks on the GCR registry.

findings.title: Dockerfile missing HEALTHCHECK instruction
findings.scenario: The Dockerfile has no HEALTHCHECK directive. Container orchestrators (Docker Compose, Kubernetes) cannot distinguish a hung or poisoned container from a healthy one, delaying incident detection and automated remediation.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Add HEALTHCHECK CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/ || exit 1

findings.title: Dockerfile uses --unsafe-perm flag
findings.scenario: The --unsafe-perm flag causes npm to run postinstall lifecycle scripts as root during the Docker build. If any dependency is compromised, the attacker's postinstall code executes with full root privileges inside the build container, potentially writing malicious artifacts into the image.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Remove --unsafe-perm; use non-root user for npm install

findings.title: Dockerfile npm install without --ignore-scripts
findings.scenario: The production npm install in the Dockerfile does not use --ignore-scripts. Any compromised transitive dependency can execute arbitrary postinstall code during the Docker build phase, injecting backdoors or exfiltrating build secrets.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Use `npm ci --ignore-scripts` and audit required postinstalls separately

findings.title: GitHub Actions workflow missing top-level permissions block
findings.scenario: The CI/CD Pipeline workflow (ci.yml) has no explicit permissions block. The GITHUB_TOKEN therefore inherits the repository default, which is commonly write-all. Any compromised step or action in this workflow could push code, create releases, or approve PRs.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Add `permissions: { contents: read }` at workflow root; elevate per-job only where needed

findings.title: GitHub Actions workflow missing top-level permissions block
findings.scenario: The Release Pipeline workflow (release.yml) has no explicit top-level permissions block. The GITHUB_TOKEN defaults to write-all, meaning any compromised action in the release pipeline can push tags, modify releases, or alter repository content.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Add `permissions: { contents: read }` at workflow root; elevate per-job only where needed

findings.title: Third-party GitHub Action not pinned to commit SHA
findings.scenario: The 'coverallsapp/github-action' action is pinned to the mutable tag '@v2'. A compromised publisher can retroactively modify this tag to execute malicious code within the CI pipeline, with access to GITHUB_TOKEN and any other workflow secrets.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Pin every third-party action to a full 40-char commit SHA

findings.title: Third-party GitHub Action not pinned to commit SHA
findings.scenario: Three steps in codeql-analysis.yml use 'github/codeql-action/...' at the mutable '@v3' tag. Although github/ namespace is first-party, tag-based references are still mutable. A tag reassignment or org-level compromise would allow arbitrary code execution in the security scanning pipeline with security-events: write scope.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Pin every third-party action to a full 40-char commit SHA

findings.title: Dependabot not configured for npm ecosystem
findings.scenario: No Dependabot configuration file exists. The npm dependency graph (450+ packages) receives no automated security update alerts. Known-vulnerable packages persist indefinitely until manually identified and updated.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Create .github/dependabot.yml with npm, github-actions, and docker ecosystem entries

findings.title: Dependabot not configured for github-actions ecosystem
findings.scenario: Without Dependabot covering GitHub Actions, SHA-pinned third-party actions will never receive automated update PRs when new versions are released. Actions can accumulate known vulnerabilities silently.

findings.title: Dependabot not configured for docker ecosystem
findings.scenario: The Dockerfile is present but no Dependabot docker ecosystem entry exists. Base image updates (node:24, gcr.io/distroless/nodejs24-debian13) containing security patches are not surfaced automatically.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Add docker ecosystem entry to .github/dependabot.yml to track Dockerfile base image updates

findings.title: package-lock.json disabled — lockfile integrity not enforced
findings.scenario: The .npmrc file sets 'package-lock=false', disabling lockfile generation. Without package-lock.json, each 'npm install' can resolve different transitive dependency versions. Supply-chain attacks (dependency confusion, malicious version bumps) become undetectable because there is no integrity baseline to compare against.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Remove 'package-lock=false' from .npmrc; commit package-lock.json; use `npm ci` in CI

findings.evidence_snippet: "postinstall": "cd frontend && npm install && cd .. && npm run build:frontend && (npm run --silent build:server || cd .)"
findings.title: postinstall script present in package.json
findings.scenario: A postinstall hook is defined in package.json. This hook executes automatically on every 'npm install' including in CI and developer environments. Any contributor who can modify package.json can inject arbitrary commands that execute with the caller's privileges. Additionally, the hook triggers a nested 'npm install' in frontend/ which recursively runs frontend postinstall hooks without --ignore-scripts protection.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Remove postinstall if possible; else document why required and use --ignore-scripts in CI

findings.evidence_snippet: (no cosign, attest-build-provenance, or sigstore reference found in release.yml)
findings.title: Container images published without signing or provenance attestation
findings.scenario: The release pipeline pushes multi-platform Docker images to DockerHub for every tagged release but includes no cosign signing step and no SLSA provenance attestation. Downstream users cannot verify the published image originated from this repository's CI pipeline rather than a compromised registry intermediary.
findings.recommended_mitigation_title: Add cosign signing step to release workflow or use actions/attest-build-provenance
